content_en:
Al-Isabah documents the lives of over twelve thousand individuals identified as Companions of the Prophet. The entries range from the most prominent and well-documented Companions — Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, the Mothers of the Believers, the major Ansar and Muhajirun — to obscure individuals known only from a single report in a genealogical register or a chain of transmission. This breadth is one of the work's great virtues: it reveals the extent and diversity of the Companion community in a way that no other single source makes visible.

For the major Companions, Ibn Hajar's entries are detailed biographical accounts: he records the person's full name and lineage, their early life, their conversion to Islam, their role in key events (Badr, Uhud, Khandaq, the conquest of Mecca, the ridda wars), the hadith they transmitted, and their death. He adjudicates between contradictory accounts in the earlier literature, drawing on his unmatched command of the hadith and biographical traditions.

For minor Companions known from a single source, the entry may be brief — a name, a note about the source of the identification, and a brief remark on reliability. These entries are no less important: they document the full extent of the Companion community and provide the evidence that Ibn Hajar uses to resolve questions about who counts as a Companion.

The entries on women Companions are particularly valuable. Earlier biographical literature had often been less comprehensive in its treatment of women, and Ibn Hajar's systematic inclusion of women Companions — the Mothers of the Believers, the daughters of Abu Bakr and Umar, the women of the Ansar who pledged allegiance at Aqaba, and hundreds of others — makes al-Isabah an essential source for the history of Muslim women in the Prophetic era. The entries document their roles as transmitters of hadith, as legal authorities on matters of domestic and family life, and as active participants in the early Islamic community.
